speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I didn't have the modern conveyances that they have nowadays. They had the...just...worked out of character. I never knew anything to do with it. I didn't have the modern conveyances that they have nowadays.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I was thinking about how they cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they all have wood stoves?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yeah. Some of them had nothing with these wood stoves. They had four legs on each other and they set them high from the floor. And they had four legs on each other and they set them high from the floor. I didn't know that it burned cold and they didn't have any much blood on their heads. Those were shipped in. My mother always used that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Or she used wood. That's about all I know of. But anyway, the other chicks like her. The building of their building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she have the fire going all day?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, whenever she cooked. Something like baking bread. Oh, I think you know, the women nowadays, they didn't know how to bake a baker's bread. They didn't know what it was. Only once in a while, always. Oh, Dad's taking us to a restaurant, but we didn't have no restaurant them days. Only hotels, if you stayed in the hotel, you'd eat in the hotel dining room, but that's all. And taking us to a dining room or hotel, why, a hotel dining room, that was a treat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be in the hotel in town?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a very nice place to eat?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes. Small little hotel.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They had small hotels and then nice big places, of course. And then they had tablecloths. They didn't know what eating on the plain board table like it is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to go out to eat very much in those days?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Not very often. My mother, she did all the cooking and everything like that. We did the outside, milking cows and feeding calves and things like that. Herding hogs and I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was all women's work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, yeah, the women, they didn't think that was anything. No. You can't do that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Today you think of that as men's, just the work that men would do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would think of women doing that kind of work, would you?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I don't know. We didn't think much about it. I was about two years old, I remember all that.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Out near the shed and she's out taking care of the cows, helping take care of the cows. I got near a calf and he knocked me down and I started crying and the cows started devolving. Because of the car commotion. I wasn't afraid of anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You weren't when you were growing up?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I wasn't afraid of animals. Never.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid then, did you spend, as a child, did you spend a lot of time with the livestock, the animals?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, we didn't think of anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother concerned about you growing up to be a little lady?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, yes. We could be a lady and be our own animals, too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She was frail, little one herself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that the work that she had to do was hard on her because she was small?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, we kept small, little one, you know, and she worked like everything.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: When she was a child at home, she had three brothers and one adopted brother and then

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: they took care of all the stuff on the outside. But when she married, then she had to learn how to feed calves and things like that, you know. We had a lot of work for her. Then we were kids, we learned how. My sister got on the outside of the fence, you know, and we helped the cow through the fence. And the cows, the calves were easy to rake into milk because we had to feed them in the calves, you know. They were tame and everything, so we learned how to learn how to milk them because they were tame. And then from then on, while they grew up, we broke them into milking.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And then later on, why?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Dad, he didn't buy them the cows anymore. He let us girls milk them. Mother had a square place in the cellar, she had a milk cellar with all the milk, so she kept a little milk in there. And a bill of rock and a mortar and all. And she kept all the milk pans in there and she just gave them all that cream all for the air. And they could make the butter and salad and take the milk to the hogs and skim milk. We stayed home all the time, we didn't know any different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say you stayed home all the time, you mean that you didn't go around visiting other places and that kind of thing?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, we enjoyed ourselves at home, we didn't know the difference. Kids didn't know, kids didn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you, then, did you have much chance to play with other kids outside the family?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, they'd come visit us, you know, neighbors and kids that way. It wasn't very many, very, very many days anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, when you were growing up, did you have a lot of different kinds of clothes or just a few pairs, a few outfits?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, we didn't have many clothes, no.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Mother bought the material and she'd make our clothes.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, you go to town, us kids are going in on the snow, how'd our mother go in?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She'd buy the material and she'd take it home and make clothes for us girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you, I was thinking about the kind of clothes that you wore, were they real long dresses and that kind of thing?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, we went according to styles, you know, them days, you know, the work was just very long, wasn't dressed in all the wool and needs and we didn't know what coveralls were, you know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Wasn't really like to wear coveralls, I don't know, dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have dresses that you wore only for work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We had dresses for work and then we'd go to church so they wore some of their dress. We made a nice material and she sewed off on my eyes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I've heard it said that most women had one good black dress, is that true, will you remember it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, sometimes they had a black dress and mother, she never liked black, she never wore navy blue, serge.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She was always so nice and so nice for us girls. She went to a program, her mother's average is quite young, she was a program school children, so all the dress patterns cut back chart. Not very gay people, we had to come home, it's not healthy, it's just like all of the art.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Were you expected, Mrs. Jones, to do much work around the house or were you figured, were the girls...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, we used to play an awful lot and us children used to play an awful lot and we would help around the house and do things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of things would you play when you were playing?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We'd play house. Then we'd play with our horses and all, I don't know.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And we didn't have the things to play with, toys and things, we didn't know what toys were, we just had a few things and we were satisfied with them. And we'd make mud pies and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did kids fight with each other the way they do nowadays?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, they used to spank in school and we didn't know what fighting was, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think you went to school when you went there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, I never got to start school until I was about nine years old. And then when I started eating, it was kind of hard for me, but I mean, I had a hard life, I liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait a minute, why was this, why did you wait until nine to start?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, Dad was careless.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: We moved to town in Maryland from there to school then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a school near you in the country where you went?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, it was a parochial school and it was a public school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, in Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to parochial school?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yes, part time.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Most of the time girls came from the water, but I don't think they came from the ocean, so I'll show you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm still wondering how it was that you didn't go out in the country, wasn't there a school nearby there?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, it was too far from each side, you see, the school, after Harley done away with them, no. They, we had to walk through school, you know, and then they got rid of the country schools and they just had the country schools and districts. And then the public school in Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how far away were you in the country from the school?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Two and three quarter miles.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: And we'd have to walk there in the morning and we'd walk there in the evening. We'd be a little tired too.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So you didn't even go, you didn't go until you were nine.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your sisters?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: They were younger than I was. I don't know much about how far they were in school or anything.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Children those days didn't go to school until late, until early.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, at nine, did you have to start in first grade?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yes, but they had different methods, seven days at grade school and then they had the grade school and we had this, we started in little ABC books and then worked ourselves up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, does that mean that you caught up before very long?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it bothering you to talk to your kids?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I was in the country and of course when we were there Hawaii, we went to school and just continued just the same as now. And we got through and now we're not to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you started to go out to work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you mean after school, in the afternoons or after you finished going to school, when you stopped going?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I was, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you worked out?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of work was that that you'd be doing for other people?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I cooked rough cakes in Walthamstow and then I worked at a table and I worked out twelve years before I was married.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I'm curious about the kind of work that you would do working out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you keep house for people?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: No, I never did any scrubbing or cleaning. I did restaurant work mostly, counter work. Like cooking hotcakes and waffles and answering the telephone, playing cashier.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Spokane this was?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that work?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I liked just clean work. I didn't have to put my hands on no dirty work or anything. Dishes and food and that's what it was all.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Meet the public and answer the telephone, pay bills and do things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of living in Spokane?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, I liked it. It was alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you happen to meet him?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, he came in while I worked. I'm sorry to interrupt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he come from down here? Oh really?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: The wind is blowing.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah. He died just last week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go together for very long before you came?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, two or three years.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Not that long I guess. Must have been about that long. Two or three years anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you happen to move back down to Genesee?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, my daddy gave the proposition so I took it up and we stayed there, ready to craft. I raised lots of poultry and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say proposition, what do you mean? He offered you to work on some of his land or what?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, there's lots of buildings and things there. But he raised the crops and I raised the poultry and calves and milk cows and I made butter and solvents. It was a whole hard work.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: You said your grandfather picked out your father for your mother. He picked him out for her. Is that the...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, that's what they used to do in the early days. Mother was just a child and she was sixteen in October and they married in July. She was just a little frail girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that she didn't have any say so? He just told, said you're going to marry this fellow?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Oh, then they... Big enough at six or seven to milk cows. Well, the general knew that we didn't have a boy's job. Dad always handed back to her three times to finish milking. We had to go back and put a milking on.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: So, but we made it. I wish she had kept track of what she made, but she didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kept track of what she made? What do you mean?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, what she made out of butter and all. How much butter she sold and things like that. She never bought it.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Then don't worry about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say she made a lot?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah, she paid the grocery bill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that? She paid the grocery bill?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Most of the time. But her age, she's a good butter maker. She had all her good luck making butter. She just seemed to come and match it for her. Well, she was Danish in the day. People were going to make butter, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Danish?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Born in Denmark. Or not born in Denmark, but she grew up. Her parents were born in Denmark and then she had...

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Had it covered on paper and she was born in the shoes and raised in this country.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: She's my girl with two brothers. And her mother died.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Then he... Then him and the boys continued to the ranch and he took her when she was 11 years old to the pro -kill school at this ranch. And there she was five years and then she came out. And she was just past 16 when she got married. My grandfather thought that I was afraid of getting married. She was afraid of a little woman.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: That's thick, isn't it?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I cooked off cake. She'd take some waffles. Three years before I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three years? In Spokane?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Yeah.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: In a place up there called Saddle Rock. And we had a big business up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Saddle Rock?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: It was the name of the restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the people that ran it up there before you went? How'd you meet them?

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: Well, I went up there to work and I asked them for work and they gave me work and they asked myself up.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I got along fine.

speaker: Agnes Healy Jones
words: I married my husband and I married my ranch and I raised poultry and stuff like that.
